metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit pre-A3 store snapshot / backup step and a documented rollback path (e.g., restore from snapshot) before the live remediation runs.
findings.finding: The live-store remediation (A3) is an irreversible operation on ~2,400 corrupted entries with no backup/snapshot step or rollback path specified.
findings.location: Band A / Success Criteria A / AC: A3
findings.suggested_fix: Before A3 executes, take a point-in-time copy of the tickets branch (e.g., `git tag pre-a3-remediation`) and document a rollback procedure (`git reset --hard pre-a3-remediation`) so the store can be restored if remediation mis-fires.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the numeric value of N (the minimum consecutive clean-pass count) required before the Phase-3 baseline consumer swap is authorized — as a named config key (`reconciler.baseline_dual_write_min_clean_passes`) or a documented constant — so the B1 gate condition is unambiguous.
findings.finding: The Phase-3 baseline consumer-swap gate references '≥ N consecutive clean passes' as its decision rule, but N is never defined anywhere in the plan, the config schema, or the codebase — there is no config key, constant, or numeric value that an executor (human or agent) can read to determine when the swap is authorized.
findings.location: Band B / B1 AC; docs/jira-sync-setup.md:434; src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/baseline_shadow.py (module docstring)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `baseline_dual_write_min_clean_passes: int = 10` (or similar) to ReconcilerConfig in `_config_schema.py` and reference it in `baseline_shadow.py`; update `docs/jira-sync-setup.md` to state the concrete default and override path.

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify C4's technical approach for 'bulk-path 429 Retry-After': explicitly state whether the fix pivots the `search_issues` bulk fetch to a direct REST endpoint (where urllib can observe Retry-After) or adopts an alternative signal (ACLI stderr parsing), and add a spike or approach decision before committing the full C4 implementation.
findings.finding: C4's goal — 'bulk-path 429 honors Retry-After' — is not technically achievable as described for the subprocess-based ACLI bulk search path. `jira workitem search --paginate` is invoked via `_run_acli` (subprocess), which cannot observe HTTP response headers including Retry-After. The existing Retry-After handling in `_call_with_backoff` applies only to urllib/REST paths. The plan conflates the two transport layers without acknowledging that the subprocess CLI path cannot surface 429 headers to the Python caller.
findings.location: Band C / Latent reconciler gaps: 'C4 (bulk-path 429 honors Retry-After)' and Success Criterion C

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC to a713: 'When baseline is absent (degrade path), the reconciler emits an observable signal (log/alert) rather than silently local-winning — or explicitly records this as a known limitation with a follow-up ticket.'
findings.finding: a713 (C1) states the conflict detection 'sharpens once B1 makes the per-binding baseline the live arbiter,' but C1's ACs do not acknowledge or bound the degraded pre-B1 behavior — the window where the absent-baseline degrade silently expands the clobber window is not covered by any AC or regression test in C1.
findings.location: child a713-b4e0-4662-4e6f (C1) — AC section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to a713's ACs: '[ ] Absent-baseline degrade: local-wins with a logged BRIDGE_ALERT noting no baseline available for conflict check (mirrors the unmapped-status alert pattern), so the degrade is never fully silent.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify ownership of the shared fsck-repair / snapshot-rebuild machinery: either A2 (36d1) builds it and A3 (34b1) calls it, or define an explicit interface contract in one child's scope and reference it in the other.
findings.finding: The 'fsck --repair' rebuild machinery has circular ownership: 36d1 (A2) states it reuses 'A3's rebuild path,' while 34b1 (A3) states it reuses 'A2's rebuild machinery.' Neither child claims to be the primary implementer — an owned-by-none gap on the shared rebuild component.
findings.location: child 36d1-0f63-cae2-4b4c (A2) and child 34b1-e19c-35be-4392 (A3)
findings.suggested_fix: Assign the rebuild path implementation to 36d1 (A2) — it is the closer fit (snapshot rebuild is a replay concern). Add to 36d1's scope: 'Expose a `rebuild_snapshot(ticket_id)` API usable by A3.' Add to 34b1's scope: 'Calls 36d1's `rebuild_snapshot` API; does NOT re-implement rebuild logic.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the A2 scope statement: remove the forward reference to 'A3's rebuild path' and instead state that A2 PROVIDES the rebuild path that A3 will consume — consistent with the A2->A3 ordering.
findings.finding: The declared A1->A2->A3 dependency order is violated by A2 (36d1) forward-referencing A3's rebuild path: A2 says it reuses 'A3's rebuild path,' which cannot exist until A3 is implemented — but A3 is declared downstream of A2.
findings.location: child 36d1-0f63-cae2-4b4c (A2 scope) vs. parent dependency edge A2->A3
findings.suggested_fix: Change 36d1 scope to: 'Provides the fsck-triggered snapshot-rebuild API (shared with A3). Does NOT depend on A3.' Change 34b1 scope to: 'Calls A2's snapshot-rebuild API to drive live remediation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before committing to C4's approach, verify that the ACLI CLI subprocess actually surfaces a `Retry-After` value in its stdout/stderr output on 429 responses (or confirm the fix will route bulk calls through the REST client instead, which already handles `Retry-After` natively).
findings.finding: Band C item C4 asserts that the ACLI-subprocess bulk path lacks Retry-After parsing, and the fix will add parity with the REST path's Retry-After handling. However, the plan does not verify that ACLI's subprocess (a JVM CLI tool) actually surfaces a Retry-After value in its stdout/stderr output in a parseable form — the REST path parses it from urllib HTTPError headers, but the subprocess path receives ACLI's own formatted output. This is an unverified capability assertion about an external tool's output format.
findings.location: Context / Latent reconciler gaps (band C) — 'coarse bulk-path 429 handling'; Success Criteria C / 'bulk-path 429 honors Retry-After'
findings.suggested_fix: Check ACLI CLI documentation or run a probe call against a rate-limited endpoint to inspect subprocess output; if Retry-After is not surfaced, re-scope C4 as 'route bulk-path calls through the REST client' rather than 'parse Retry-After from subprocess output'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign ownership of the Risk #5 acceptance record (single-hop status transitions explicitly out of scope) to one child (e.g., d01e scope doc, or a new micro-task) so the parent AC can be closed.
findings.finding: No child explicitly owns or records the parent AC 'Risk #5 (single-hop status transitions) explicitly accepted / out of scope.' The parent acceptance checklist requires this to be a verifiable deliverable, but no child carries an AC item, ADR reference, or scope note that discharges it.
findings.location: parent AC: 'Risk #5 (single-hop status transitions) explicitly accepted / out of scope' — not present in any child
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC to d01e (or a118) — e.g., '[ ] ADR or validation-matrix scope section explicitly records single-hop status transitions as accepted / out of scope' — so the parent checklist item has a concrete child-level deliverable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define A3's rollback/back-out path and explicitly assert the post-rollback store state: either (a) the repair is purely additive (compensating-forward, *.retired style) so rollback leaves the store in its pre-repair state, or (b) a snapshot/backup of the tickets branch is taken before A3 begins and is the defined rollback target; attach a DATA LOSS RISK note if neither is guaranteed.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the post-rollback schema state of the store after a partial A3 remediation. If A3 is partially applied and then rolled back (e.g., the repair strategy proves incorrect), the store will be in a mixed state — some tickets repaired, some not — without any plan assertion that this mixed state is safe or how to reach a clean baseline.
findings.location: AC: A3 / Success Criteria A / Scope — no rollback section exists
findings.suggested_fix: Before A3 executes, require a tagged backup commit of the tickets branch tip (e.g., `refs/backups/pre-a3-repair`). Define rollback as: reset the branch to that tag. Assert that no reconciler writes are accepted between the backup tag and the start of the repair sweep.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a gate to d01e: 'Three-way-merge validation (B1 surface) MUST run after a118's consumer flag is confirmed flipped; record the a118 pass ID at which the flag was flipped alongside D1 results.'
findings.finding: D1 (d01e) has no declared dependency on a118 (B1) being fully live, but D1's matrix requires validating three-way-merge behavior against the live per-binding-baseline arbiter (B1). If D1 runs before a118's consumer flag is flipped, it validates shadow/pre-swap behavior, not the B1 live state — an ordering gap.
findings.location: child d01e-d48d-de62-4833 (D1) and child a118-fa8e-547a-49ba (B1)
findings.suggested_fix: In d01e's ACs, add: '[ ] B1 convergence surface validated only after a118 consumer flag is live (not shadow); cite a118 flip timestamp in results.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a cross-reference in both a713 and acd0: 'Coordinate merge order with the sibling child modifying apply_outbound.py; review combined diff before merging both.'
findings.finding: a713 (C1) and acd0 (C3) both modify `apply_outbound.py` with no coordination note between them — an implicit shared-state risk in a reconciler hot path.
findings.location: child a713-b4e0-4662-4e6f (C1) and child acd0-852a-a5e5-4738 (C3)
findings.suggested_fix: In a713's scope, add: 'Coordinate with acd0 (C3) on apply_outbound.py edits.' In acd0's scope, add: 'Coordinate with a713 (C1) on apply_outbound.py edits.' Optionally sequence C3 before C1 since C3's lines (117-124) are upstream of C1's (207-232).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that Band B (Phase-3 convergence consumer swap, originating from epic file-lens-jewel) is intentionally bundled here rather than tracked as a dependency from its source epic — and verify that A-band store remediation can ship independently if B is delayed.
findings.finding: Band B (Phase-3 convergence consumer swap) originates from a separate, already-named epic (file-lens-jewel) and represents architectural feature-completion work, giving this epic a second reason-to-change (completing a convergence design) alongside its primary reason (fixing store corruption and reconciler bugs). While the coupling through C1 is cited, if the convergence work is delayed or blocked, it will stall the entire epic including the independently-valuable Band A store remediation.
findings.location: Context section ('Convergence redesign (landed, epic file-lens-jewel; band B)') and AC line 'Band B: B1 (Phase-3 baseline consumer swap)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the A2 description, enumerate the candidate approaches for RC2b (e.g., epoch-bump on union-merge, horizon extension, replay positional-skip rewrite) and state the selection criterion or the already-preferred option so the executor can act without stopping.
findings.finding: AC A2 says the child ticket 'carries an approach decision' for fixing the RC2b sub-horizon append, but the plan does not state candidate approaches or the criteria for choosing among them — an executor must invent the design from scratch.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Band A: 'A2 (RC2b horizon-safe; carries an approach decision)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection under A2: 'Candidate approaches: (1) bump HLC on every union-merge to exceed peer snapshot horizon; (2) widen replay horizon to include sub-horizon appends from known peers; (3) reorder positional skip to occur after horizon check. Preferred: (1) unless write amplification exceeds X%. Final approach decision must be recorded in the child ticket before implementation begins.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'comprehensive live validation' in D1 with an explicit enumerated checklist of the bidirectional flows to be exercised (e.g., create, update, field-conflict, hard-delete→re-create, 429 recovery, baseline-swap) so the criterion is deterministically evaluable.
findings.finding: D1's success criterion uses 'comprehensive' to describe the live-validation surface without enumerating what operations or flows are included, making it subjective and not deterministically evaluable.
findings.location: Success Criteria — Band D: 'D1 (comprehensive live validation + guaranteed bilateral cleanup)' and Acceptance Criteria — 'D1 relates_to B1/C1/C2/C3/C4'
findings.suggested_fix: State D1 as: 'Live validation covers at minimum: (1) create ticket local→Jira, (2) update ticket both directions with field conflict, (3) hard-delete→re-create round-trip, (4) 429 Retry-After honored during bulk path, (5) per-binding baseline swap (B1 flow). Agent reports pass/fail per flow. All test artifacts removed from both Jira and the local store before session ends.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in C3 the concrete observable artifact for field-allowlist drops (e.g., 'a structured WARN log entry including field name, ticket ID, and direction is emitted and present in the session run log') so the criterion is deterministically evaluable.
findings.finding: C3's success criterion 'field-allowlist drops observable' does not specify the observable artifact — log entry, metric, alert, or store annotation — making it outcome-vague.
findings.location: Success Criteria — Band C: 'field-allowlist drops observable'; Acceptance Criteria: 'C3 (field-allowlist alert)'
findings.suggested_fix: State the default: 'field-allowlist drops emit a structured WARN log entry (level WARN, fields: ticket_id, field_name, direction) visible in the reconciler run log. No UI/metric required for this band.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define in C1 the concrete artifact for 'clobber conflict signal' (e.g., 'a CONFLICT_DETECTED event is appended to the ticket store with both-sides values' or 'a structured WARN log entry') so the criterion is deterministically evaluable in-session.
findings.finding: C1's success criterion 'true both-sides field conflicts recorded' does not specify what 'recorded' means — log entry, conflict marker in the store, a new event type — so an executor cannot determine the required artifact.
findings.location: Success Criteria — Band C: 'true both-sides field conflicts recorded'; Acceptance Criteria: 'C1 (clobber conflict signal)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief justification in the plan body (or in the AC dependency note) explaining *why* A1 must precede A2, or remove the A1->A2 edge if the ordering is not actually required.
findings.finding: The AC wires dependency A1->A2 (compaction *.retired fix must precede RC2b horizon-safe fix), but the Context describes these as two independent bugs with no stated causal relationship. A1 fixes a compaction rename invariant; A2 fixes a concurrent-write sub-horizon append. No explanation in the plan body establishes why A1 must complete before A2 can be implemented or validated.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'Dependency edges wired: A1->A2, A1->A3, A2->A3'
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is that a corrected compaction pass must run (A1) before the store is in a state where the RC2b horizon logic is meaningful (e.g. A2's fix touches compaction-adjacent replay paths), state that explicitly. If the two fixes are truly independent, change the AC to 'A1->A3, A2->A3' and drop A1->A2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend B1 (or add an explicit sub-criterion) to verify that the old/redundant arbiter is *removed or disabled*, not just that the new baseline arbiter is live — satisfying the migration criterion that both removal and replacement are confirmed.
findings.finding: The Context states 'two arbiters encode the rule twice' as a known defect in the convergence redesign (band B), but no AC item verifies that the redundant/old arbiter is *removed* — B1 only verifies the Phase-3 consumer swap is live and validated. This is a migration-completeness gap: the criterion covers replacement but not removal.
findings.location: Context — 'two arbiters encode the rule twice'; Acceptance Criteria — B1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to B1's description: 'and the legacy direction-arbiter code path is deleted/disabled, confirmed by grep/test that only one arbiter is reachable at runtime'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document an explicit rollback/re-shadow mechanism for B1 (e.g., a feature flag or config toggle that re-enables SHADOW mode) so the consumer swap can be reversed without a full redeploy.
findings.finding: The Phase-3 consumer swap (B1) flips the authoritative direction arbiter but specifies no rollback path if the new baseline produces incorrect arbitration in production.
findings.location: Band B / Success Criteria B / AC: B1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one ADR (or extend an existing one) covering the new contracts introduced by C1-C4: conflict signal shape, hard-delete re-create semantics, field-allowlist alert interface, and 429 Retry-After behavior.
findings.finding: Band C behavioral changes (C1 conflict signal, C2 hard-delete re-create path, C3 field-allowlist alert, C4 429 Retry-After) introduce new contracts/patterns with no ADR or doc artifact mentioned.
findings.location: Band C / Success Criteria C / AC: C1-C4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Require A2 to produce or reference an ADR capturing the approach decision for RC2b horizon-safe appends, including the alternatives considered and why they were rejected.
findings.finding: A2's 'approach decision' implies a new architectural choice for handling sub-horizon concurrent appends, but no ADR or design-doc artifact is mentioned for it.
findings.location: Band A / Context (RC2b) / AC: A2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concurrency spike or RED fixture for A2 that exercises the stale-base / sub-horizon HLC scenario under at least two concurrent writers and asserts that replay does NOT drop the appended event, so the approach decision is empirically validated before full implementation.
findings.finding: The RC2b horizon-safe append fix (A2) addresses a concurrent-write race — a stale-base write with an HLC below a peer's snapshot horizon being silently dropped by replay's positional skip — but the plan calls for no spike, RED fixture, or concurrency benchmark to empirically validate that the chosen approach actually eliminates the race before full build-out. 'Carries an approach decision' implies the design is still open; correctness of HLC-ordering under concurrent writers plus replay safety cannot be established by reasoning alone.
findings.location: Context section (RC2b description) and Acceptance Criteria — Band A: A2 (RC2b horizon-safe; carries an approach decision)
findings.suggested_fix: Before committing to the A2 approach, write a deterministic unit fixture that: (1) creates a snapshot at horizon H, (2) appends an event with HLC = H-ε on a stale base concurrently with a second writer, (3) runs the union-merge + replay pipeline, and (4) asserts the event survives. Run this fixture RED against the current code and GREEN against the proposed fix. Reference this fixture in the A2 ADR as proof of correctness.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the A1 section, enumerate and acknowledge all callers/consumers that currently depend on compaction hard-deleting source files (e.g., directory scanners, existence checks), and confirm or add AC entries ensuring those callers are updated or remain correct under *.retired semantics.
findings.finding: A1's compaction behavior change (from hard os.remove() to *.retired rename) is not accompanied by an acknowledgment of callers/consumers that depend on deleted files being absent after compaction, risking silent breakage in file-existence checks or directory scans.
findings.location: Context section (Invariant gap, band A) and Success Criteria A (A1 *.retired)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the A1 AC entry such as: 'All code paths that read the compacted directory or check for source file existence post-compaction are audited and confirmed correct under *.retired rename semantics (or updated to ignore *.retired files).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the A2 AC entry to state an implementation-complete done condition (e.g., 'RC2b: concurrent sub-horizon appends are not silently dropped, verified by targeted unit/integration test; approach decision document is a prerequisite artifact, not the done condition').
findings.finding: AC entry for A2 conflates a design artifact ('carries an approach decision') with an implementation done-condition, making it ambiguous whether A2 is complete when the approach is decided or when RC2b is actually fixed.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'A2 (RC2b horizon-safe; carries an approach decision)'
findings.suggested_fix: Revise to: 'A2 (RC2b horizon-safe: implemented and verified by targeted test; approach decision document produced as a prerequisite)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the chosen mechanism for A2 (RC2b horizon-safe) — e.g., 'reducer UUID-only positional skip' (change `replay_events` to use UUID-membership rather than `idx < start_idx` for pre-SNAPSHOT filtering) or 'write-path HLC timestamp-floor correction' — and add a one-sentence rationale for why it was chosen over the alternative.
findings.finding: Band A2 (RC2b horizon-safe) explicitly defers its approach to 'carries an approach decision' — no approach is stated in the plan. Without a chosen mechanism, the implementer cannot proceed and reviewers cannot assess correctness. The two structurally viable options differ significantly: (α) write-path timestamp-floor correction (guarantee the new event's HLC filename prefix > SNAPSHOT's HLC before commit, so it is never positionally skipped); (β) reducer-layer UUID-only skip guard (replace the positional `idx < start_idx` drop with a UUID-membership check, eliminating the positional-skip TOCTOU entirely). Option (β) aligns with the store's UUID-as-identity philosophy and has zero write-path blast radius.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — Band A children: 'A2 (RC2b horizon-safe; carries an approach decision)'
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt approach (β): change `replay_events` in `reducer/_processors.py` so events before the SNAPSHOT position are skipped by UUID membership in `snapshot_source_uuids` rather than by positional index. This eliminates the TOCTOU without touching the write path, composes correctly with the existing UUID-dedup guard, and requires no change to HLC or commit ordering. Add to A2's spec: 'Change `start_idx`-based positional skip to UUID-membership-only skip; a pre-SNAPSHOT event whose UUID is NOT in `source_event_uuids` is an RC2b orphan and is promoted to replay rather than dropped.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the cleanup mechanism for D1 so 'guaranteed' is an engineered property: e.g., 'the agent registers all created artifacts (Jira key + local ticket id) in a test-run manifest before creation; a finally/cleanup phase reads the manifest and deletes all artifacts regardless of how the run ended; the manifest itself is committed to the tickets branch so a re-run of cleanup is idempotent.'
findings.finding: D1's 'guaranteed cleanup of every test artifact in both Jira and the local store' is stated as a hard requirement but the plan provides no mechanism for guaranteeing cleanup survives agent failure, timeout, or crash during the live-validation run. Without a specified cleanup protocol (e.g., try/finally structure, a pre-registered cleanup list executed by a separate finalizer pass, idempotent teardown keyed on a test-run tag), 'guaranteed' is an assertion, not an engineered property — and a single agent crash leaves test artifacts permanently in both Jira and the local store.
findings.location: Context — 'Validation gap (band D)': 'Live validation is performed by an AGENT (ACLI access), with guaranteed cleanup of test artifacts in both Jira and the local store — not deferred to the operator.' / Success Criteria D: 'with GUARANTEED cleanup'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in C2's spec whether the outbound re-create must fire in the same pass (and add that as an AC) or whether it may be deferred to the next pass (and if so, specify what durable state records the re-create intent so a crash between passes does not lose it).
findings.finding: C2's success criterion ('hard-delete → re-create works') leaves unspecified whether the outbound re-create runs in the same pass or the next pass. This is a material ordering/atomicity assumption: if re-create runs in the next pass, the binding store must correctly reflect the 'needs re-create' state between passes (the `follow_on` dict is currently in-memory-only and is not persisted). The code comment at `apply_inbound.py:560` says 'in the same pass', but if that is not achieved, the re-create is silently lost on a crash between passes.
findings.location: Success Criteria C: 'C2 (hard-delete re-create)' / apply_inbound.py:560 TODO

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the A2 child ticket's approach decision, explicitly document whether adopting Lamport logical clocks (as git-bug does, per the OSS comparison's G1) was considered for the RC2b fix, and justify the chosen approach against that alternative.
findings.finding: The RC2b fix (A2) addresses a novel sub-horizon append data-loss race using a bespoke 'horizon-safe' mechanism. The OSS comparison doc (G1) and session log both identify git-bug's Lamport MemClock as a peer solution to exactly this class of cross-clone ordering problem. The plan does not document whether Lamport/vector clock adoption was considered and rejected for A2, or why a bespoke HLC-horizon fix is preferred. The A2 child 'carries an approach decision' but the parent plan names no candidate approaches.
findings.location: Context — RC2b description; Success Criteria A; AC 'A2 (RC2b horizon-safe; carries an approach decision)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit batching/throttling spec to A3: define a per-batch size (e.g., N tickets per commit), a pause interval between batches, and a reconciler-pause or quiesce window so the repair sweep does not race with live reconciler writes.
findings.finding: A3 live-store remediation (driving to zero fsck errors across ~2,400 corrupted issues) specifies no batching or throttling. The reconciler is described as 'the busiest concurrent writer' running every 20 minutes; a single unthrottled repair pass over the full corrupted set risks contention, lock-ups, or creating new corruption during the sweep.
findings.location: Success Criteria A / AC: A3 / Context RC1 ('~2,400-issue store corruption')
findings.suggested_fix: Specify that A3 runs in chunks (e.g., 100 tickets per repair commit), with a configurable inter-batch sleep, and that the reconciler CI is paused (or its branch locked read-only) for the duration of the A3 sweep. Add this as an explicit sub-step in the A3 AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that A3 remediation is resumable and idempotent: define a progress-tracking mechanism (e.g., a repair-cursor commit or a per-ticket 'repaired' tag) so that a re-run skips already-corrected tickets without double-applying fixes.
findings.finding: A3 remediation is not specified as resumable or idempotent. If the repair run is interrupted mid-sweep (e.g., CI timeout, agent crash, or a reconciler write conflict), re-running it is not guaranteed to be safe — partial repairs applied once could be double-applied on retry.
findings.location: AC: A3 / Success Criteria A

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and implement an unconditional cleanup mechanism for D1's live-validation agent (e.g., `trap cleanup EXIT` + a cleanup function that deletes both the Jira issue and local ticket regardless of exit path), so that 'GUARANTEED cleanup' is mechanically enforceable, not just aspirational.
findings.finding: D1's 'GUARANTEED cleanup' of test artifacts is asserted but the existing e2e probe script (e2e_validation_probe.sh) uses `set -euo pipefail` with cleanup in a final Phase 6 block and no `trap cleanup EXIT`. An early-phase failure (e.g., Phase 1 Jira create error) causes the script to exit before cleanup runs, leaving orphaned Jira issues and local tickets — contradicting the guarantee. The plan does not specify the mechanism by which cleanup is made unconditional.
findings.location: Success Criteria D / AC D1 ('GUARANTEED cleanup of every test artifact in both Jira and the local store') and Band D description ('guaranteed cleanup … not deferred to the operator')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `cleanup()` function at the top of the probe script (or agent equivalent) that calls `client.delete_issue($JIRA_KEY)` and `rebar delete $LOCAL_ID --user-approved`, and register it with `trap cleanup EXIT` immediately after `LOCAL_ID` and `JIRA_KEY` are set. This makes cleanup unconditional regardless of which phase fails.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access posture for D1 live validation: specify whether test artifact writes are scoped to a dedicated sandbox/test Jira project (preferred) or the production project with rationale, and document how cleanup failure is handled to prevent artifact leakage into production.
findings.finding: Band D (D1) live validation creates and destroys test artifacts in live Jira, but the plan does not declare whether these writes target a sandboxed Jira project/space or the production project; the access posture of the validation surface is undeclared.
findings.location: Validation gap (band D) / Success Criteria D / AC: Band D: D1
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the D1 acceptance criterion: 'Validation writes target a dedicated test project (e.g. JIRA_PROJECT=<sandbox>) or are explicitly accepted as production-project writes with documented cleanup-failure handling (e.g. idempotent re-run, operator alert).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the agent's fallback protocol when a D1/B1 cleanup sub-step fails (e.g., mandatory `trap cleanup EXIT` in the validation script, a retry policy for Jira delete, and an escalation path if cleanup cannot complete), replacing the bare 'guaranteed' assertion with an implementable mechanism.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'GUARANTEED cleanup of every test artifact in both Jira and the local store — not deferred to the operator' (D1, B1, Validation gap) but provides no fallback protocol for when a cleanup sub-step fails. The closest artifact (e2e_validation_probe.sh) uses `set -euo pipefail` with no `trap EXIT` guard, meaning any early failure leaves live Jira artifacts — the exact opposite of guaranteed. The plan does not specify what the agent must do when delete_issue or local-ticket-delete fails.
findings.location: Validation gap section ('GUARANTEED cleanup'); Success Criteria D ('guaranteed cleanup of every test artifact'); AC D1; scripts/jira-pressure-test/e2e_validation_probe.sh Phase 6
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `cleanup()` function called via `trap cleanup EXIT` to e2e_validation_probe.sh (mirroring the pattern in scripts/probe-rebar.sh:124) and document in the plan that 'guaranteed' means: cleanup runs unconditionally via EXIT trap, retries once on transient API error, and logs a CLEANUP_FAILED sentinel if it still cannot delete — escalating to operator only for that residual case.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the D1 validation surface as an explicit enumeration (e.g., a checklist or table: which field types × which directions × which edge cases), so the agent has a concrete schema of what 'comprehensive live validation' means and reviewers can verify completeness.
findings.finding: D1's 'full bidirectional surface' validation has no enumerated test matrix or operation schema. The plan says the agent must validate 'the full bidirectional surface' but provides no list of which field types, operation classes, or edge cases constitute completeness. An agent executor cannot determine when D1 is done.
findings.location: Success Criteria D; AC D1 ('comprehensive live validation + guaranteed bilateral cleanup')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC to d01e: 'Three-way-merge (B1) behavior validated against the live consumer-swapped baseline arbiter (not shadow), with results recorded and test artifacts cleaned up' — and add a dependency note in d01e: 'Requires a118 consumer flag live.'
findings.finding: B1's 'agent performs its live validation with cleanup' requirement is split across two children with no explicit handoff: a118 disavows agent execution ('CANNOT run in an agent session'), and d01e covers live validation but does not explicitly name itself as the owner of the B1-validation remainder — a partial-without-remainder gap.
findings.location: child a118-fa8e-547a-49ba (B1 code) and child d01e-d48d-de62-4833 (D1 validation)
findings.suggested_fix: In d01e's 'What' or AC section, add: 'Validates B1 (per-binding baseline as live arbiter, consumer flag flipped) — requires a118 fully live.' In a118's ACs, add: '[ ] B1 live-validation delegated to D1 (d01e); D1 must run against the post-swap state.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
